title: ComfyUI Tutorial Series: Ep15 - Styles Update, Prompts from File & Batch Images
description:
Welcome to episode 15 of our ComfyUI tutorial series! In this video, we explore how to enhance your workflows by adding custom styles, running multiple prompts from a file, batch loading images from a folder, and more exciting features.

Here's what you'll learn:

- How to install and use the ComfyUI itools custom node.
- Creating and using custom styles stored in YAML files.
- Running multiple prompts from a text file.
- Batch loading images and applying text overlays.
- Best practices for creating and editing your style files.

This tutorial is packed with helpful tips and tricks, making it easier to organize and enhance your image generation process with ComfyUI.

Get the workflows and instructions from discord
https://discord.gg/gggpkVgBf3

Unlock exclusive perks by joining our channel:
https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCmMbwA-s3GZDKVzGZ-kPwaQ/join
00:00 Introduction
00:22 Installing the itools Custom Node
00:53 Working with Custom Styles and YAML Files
13:47 Adding Text Overlay to
transcript: Welcome to episode 15 of our Comfy UI tutorial series.  Today, I am talking about styles and how to add your own  and use them more easily in your workflows.  Then, I will discuss running multiple prompts  from a file, loading batch images from a folder,  adding text overlays to your generations, and more cool stuff. Click install and wait for a definition stalling.  Then, click restart, and OK.  You can use the clear button to clear your workflow.  Double-click on the canvas and search for iTools.  Here, you can see all the nodes available in this custom node.  Let's start with this one.  iTools prompt styler.  If I hover the mouse over the title,  you can see it says that it helps you populate your prompt  quickly using a template stored in a YAML file.  So, let's see how it actually works. Instead of none, I can select a different template,  like art deco, for example,  and then add a prompt, like a cat. So, we have a positive and a negative prompt  that comes from that art style selection. Right now, they are not active.  If you read the Red Me file,  you can see it says to move the files  you want into the styles folder  in order to activate them  and then restart a Comfy UI.  By the way, here are all the styles  split by category  and the Pixoroma file has them all in one  to be able to test it.  I will just copy all and paste them  in the styles folder.  I go to manager, restart and click OK.  After the restart,  if I go to the styles folder,  I can see all of them here.  Use the mouse wheel to scroll up and down.  If I select photography,  I can find only photography styles.  If I select cinematic and run it,  you can see how the cat is added  in that cinematic style  along with a negative prompt.  If I select painting,  I can see different painting styles.  If you select the Pixoroma file,  you can access all over 300 art styles.  You can just search for a word on top.  For example, photography will give you  all photography styles  and painting will give you painting styles  and so on, you get the idea.  Now, this node is for a single style.  If you want one with extra styles,  then you can search for itools again  and select the one that has extra in the name.  This one has four different style options  allowing you to combine up to four different styles.  By default, it loads with four different files.  Basic, camera, artist and mood.  If I select a style from those files,  you can see that they are added in order.  The first styles are more important  than the second and so on.  It's just text that comes one after the other.  If you don't want any style active,  you just set all to none.  You can load the styles that make sense  for what you want to do.  For example, I can load a painting file category  and the art category  so I can choose for painting  like digital fantasy game art  and for style like cybergoth.  This gives me an interesting combination  that will create a nice prompt  in a few clicks which can save a lot of time.  But let's see how we can implement this  in an actual workflow. In order to connect our node,  we need to convert text to input.  We do that for both positive and negative prompts  and now we just connect the positive  to the text input on positive  and the negative to the text input of negative.  That's all you need to do, quite easy, isn't it?  I am adding a simple prompt like a cat  and to be able to compare,  I will use a fixed seed.  If we test the workflow without any style,  this is what we get for the cat prompt with that fixed seed.  If I choose the cyberpunk template and test it,  the cat will look completely different. If I change to pastel, I get this pastel drawing.  If you want to see what the prompts look like,  you can add that show any node for both positive  and negative prompts. If we go to the styles folder,  you can see the default ones plus the ones I added.  My recommendation is to not edit the default ones.  These are the five artist, basic, camera, mood, and original.  Instead, make a copy and give it a different name  or create categories or file names that make sense for you. I'm not sure if all cameras are recognized in flux,  but you can use them to get subtle variations.  If I select another one,  sometimes that can be enough to fix a small mistake.  I can also make the camera more important  if I put it first, so I change the order  and place the camera first and photography  as the second style.  As you can see, it's a little different.  Some of my favorite styles are painting styles  like this digital fantasy game art style.  Then I combine it with different art styles  from the art category  by playing around with different combinations.  You can get some nice results with simple prompts  and just by switching styles.  If you like to experiment a lot like me,  then it might be faster to have a single file  instead of multiple files  that contain all the styles in one.  I can load all four styles as my pixel Roma file  and I don't need to touch that file anymore.  I only select the styles from it.  This way I can select a painting style  and an art style quickly,  only two selections instead of four  or I can select a sculpture with a craft  and get something like this.  If you don't want to use any style,  just set them all to have the value none.  I have some cool styles in the experimental one.  For example, divine portraiture  combined with a better prompt  and another style can give interesting results.  Some of the prompts need to be adapted for the style.  For example, if I want to do something interior related,  I can include an interior prompt  and then select the art styles from the list.  For example, this dream-like style always adds clouds  and grass inside interiors creating a surreal look  or this luxury interior style  makes all interiors look more luxurious.  I am now loading one of the saved SDXL workflows  that I did earlier.  This one has only one style  but you can replace it with another node  that has extra in the name  which includes multiple styles.  We know that the image is ready here  in the VAE decode node  and then we can choose to save it or preview it  using either the save or preview node.  Search for I tools again  and add the add text overlay node.  This node lets you add text on top of the image. Right now, the color code has double A after the code  which means it has alpha or transparency active.  If I remove that,  you can see it's not transparent anymore.  That is visible only when the overlay mode is active.  You can also use basic colors like blue and other colors  or you can use a color picker from Photoshop  or go on Google and use that color picker  to select the color and copy that hex value. Of course, you can do more complex stuff with it.  If I convert the text to input,  I can drag a connection  from the positive prompt to the text input.  This way, it will take that prompt  and put it on top of the image.  Of course, now it's too big.  I can either turn off overlay  or just make the font smaller.  If I set it to 20,  we can now see both the image and the prompt.  If I turn off overlay,  the text goes under the image,  the text is always white.  So I select the dark colors  or just use black  as that provides good contrast and is visible.  I will clear this canvas  and let's search for tools again.  This time, select load images. The default is set to the first four,  but I can change that to any value I want.  I have this folder with six images  so I will go to the address bar and copy the path.  Then I paste it here where it says image directory.  Okay, the node is set.  Now, what do we do with those images?  We can preview or save them.  So I add a save node.  Now, if I run that,  it just loads the images from that folder  and saves them in the output folder.  If you click on one, you can see it bigger.  And from the bottom right,  you can navigate through different images.  If I set the limit to two,  it only loads the first two images.  If I set it to six, I get six images.  I will leave it at the default value for now.  Let's add the overlay node we used earlier.  So we load the images from the folder.  Use this node to add a text overlay.  Then save them.  I add the text fixeroma and a maroon color.  I run the workflow and I get text  on all those images quite fast.  Of course, there are many ways you can use this  batch image loader, for example. If I change it to 512, it will be the bottom right.  If I put half,  I get a crop of only the center in this case.  So you need to adapt the values  based on your image sizes in the output.  You can find all those saved images.  I will load a workflow from episode 12  with the upscaler.  Now, instead of the load image node  that only loads one image,  I can add the other one from I tools  that loads more images at once  and I just redo the connection.  Now, if I try to run it, of course,  I get an error because I didn't set a path  from where to take the images.  So I copy the path and paste it  in the image directory field.  Now, if I run the workflow,  I will get an upscale for all those images.  The thing is that you will not get a preview  after each image is upscaled.  You will only see the result when all are finished.  So it's a good idea to test on one image first  by setting the limit to one.  If that works, how you want it,  then try with more images.  Here are all the images upscaled.  It seems that it works in comparer also,  but you need to compare the same number.  For example, A1 compared with B1, A2 with B2 and so on.  In the output folder, you can find all the images there.  Sometimes you might want to use a certain prompt  for image upscale or maybe you are using image to image  and want a different prompt for each image.  So I am manually adding the prompt  in the image name for each of the images.  Make sure I update my directory  since I move the images to a different folder  than where I have the positive prompt. When I run the workflow,  you can see it takes that prompt from each image  and it is used as a prompt  during the upscale process, which is quite useful.  I will load a basic SDXL workflow  from episode three again.  This time I want to show you how to run multiple prompts  from a file from the same ITools node,  add the prompt loader.  This one by default runs all the prompts from a text file.  We can find that text file if we go  to the ITools node folder  and look for examples here. However, I like to have my prompt file  somewhere that I can access easily.  So you can create a new text file  in a location that is convenient for you  and give it a name.  I will name it my prompts.  Inside you can add your prompts.  I will add five random prompts  just so we can test.  Then I save the file.  Now we need the path to that text file  in Windows 11, you can just right click  and select copy as path.  In Windows 10, you have the copy path option  in the home menu.  So I copy the path and then in the file path field,  I paste that path.  In this case, the seed represents the position line  in that text file.  Zero is the first one.  One is the second one and so on.  The troll after generate can be random  or choose increment.  Now, of course, to be able to connect,  we need to convert text to input  and then we can connect it.  You can add a show preview node  if you want to see what prompt it got from the text file.  When I run it, you can see that it loaded the first prompt  and the seed incremented to value one.  If I run it again, I get the second prompt  then the third prompt and so on.  If I change to randomize, I get a random value.  Let me show you how you can quickly get a list of prompts.  I am using chat UPT and asked for five stable diffusion prompts  for acute cartoon bunny and to not include quotes.  I received a list that I can copy and paste  into that prompts text file.  Now, it's important to remove all extra spaces  because they create new lines  which will be considered empty prompts  causing the generation of something random.  I can turn off WordRap to see the lines better  and now I can remove the spaces between them.  I save the file and let's test it.  I will leave it set to random  and it all works fine.  It gives me random prompts from that text file.  This is how I like to do it.  I set it to increment.  So I know it takes all the prompts in order  then check extra options and enable AutoCue.
